fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that reactive state container thing we've been talking about. The basic idea is devs should be able to just write normal JS like `store.user.name = 'ada'` and everything else happens automatically — no dispatch calls, no action creators, none of that Redux ceremony. When something changes, subscribers get notified with the path of what changed, and the store should do that smart memory thing we did in the immutable updater module (you know the one — like how the login module handles nested clones). Also the store object itself should never get swapped out, even if you replace everything inside it — people hold references to it.

We also need to handle when someone tries to write into a path that doesn't exist yet (like a missing parent), that should fail gracefully. Arrays should just work naturally too. And there's a replace operation that wipes everything and installs new stuff — old keys get reported as gone first, then new ones as added.

The output format for the test harness is very specific — reads, then changes, then identity probes, then snapshot — and keys in snapshots need to be sorted. Can someone also make sure the codebase isn't just one giant file? We've had complaints about that before. Ping me if the path rendering format isn't clear.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: createStore(init?: object): { store: ProxyStore, subscribe: (fn: (path: string) => void) => void }

trigger_keywords: what comes first in the printed result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output must follow a strict fixed order: first, all `get` lines in the order the get ops were requested; then exactly one `changes=[...]` line; then all `identity` probe lines in the order specified; then the `snapshot=` line. This order is mandatory regardless of op ordering.

Only the objects on the direct path from the root to the changed property are rebuilt (replaced). All sibling sub-trees and off-path objects retain their original reference (preserved). The identity probe must capture references before mutation and compare after to report `preserved` or `replaced`.

The `replace` operation must first delete all currently existing top-level properties (reported as changes in their original key order), then install all new properties from the replacement object (reported in the order they appear). The store object's own identity must remain stable — it is the same object reference before and after replacement.

If an operation requires navigating through a non-existent or non-navigable (e.g. primitive) intermediate node, the entire request must output exactly one line: `error=invalid_path` and nothing else — no changes, no snapshot, no gets.

All object keys in `snapshot=` output (and in `get` output for objects) must be rendered in sorted (lexicographic) alphabetical order. Arrays are rendered in index order. This applies recursively to all nested objects.

All paths are rendered starting with `store` as the root, e.g. `store.user.address.city` or `store.items.2`. Array indices are rendered as their numeric string segment in the dot-separated path (e.g. `store.items.1`). There is no bracket notation.

Every mutation — set or delete — is reported individually in the exact order applied. Repeated changes to the same path each produce their own entry in the changes array and are never collapsed or deduplicated.

The codebase must be organized as multiple files reflecting distinct responsibilities: proxy interception, immutable updater with structural sharing, path utilities, and subscription/notification registry. A single monolithic file is explicitly not acceptable for this domain.

Reading a property that does not exist should return `null` in the output (rendered as `null` in the get line), not undefined or an error — unless the navigation path itself is invalid (requires traversing through a primitive or missing intermediate node, which triggers `error=invalid_path`).

Assigning to the index exactly one past the current last index appends a new element and grows the array length. The change is reported with the numeric index as the final path segment. This is treated like any other property set in terms of structural sharing and notification.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that smart memory thing we did in the immutable updater module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Structural sharing: when a nested property changes, rebuild only the objects on the path from root to the changed node, reusing all off-path sub-tree references unchanged. Implemented in the immutable update module (e.g. `src/updater.js` or equivalent).

context_pointers.vague_reference: like how the login module handles nested clones
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The immutable path-update logic that shallow-clones each ancestor object on the changed path while preserving references to all sibling branches — analogous to how Redux-style reducers do selective cloning, referenced here as prior art in the codebase.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I'm not sure about the exact technical details on that one — you'd need to dig into the PRD or check the test cases for the precise contract. My understanding is just that the store should feel like a plain object to the developer while tracking everything under the hood.

feature1_set_and_read.json.description: The store is an observable object. Assigning a value to a top-level property records the property's value so it can later be read back through the same object, and reading a property returns the most recently assigned value. Each assignment is also reported to any registered change subscriber as the path of the property that changed. The adapter prints each requested read, the ordered list of reported change paths, and a final snapshot of the whole store.

cases.expected_output:
get store.count = 1
get store.name = "ada"
changes=["store.count","store.name"]
snapshot={"count":1,"name":"ada"}

feature2_nested_update.json.description: Assignments may target a property nested several levels deep inside the store. Writing to a deep property updates only that property, leaving the rest of the surrounding structure intact, and the new value is observable when the nested location is read back. A deep write may either overwrite an existing nested property or introduce a new property alongside existing siblings. The reported change path identifies the exact nested location that changed, from the root down to the written property.

feature3_delete.json.description: A property can be removed from the store entirely, whether it sits at the top level or nested inside another object. After removal the property is absent from the store snapshot while all other properties remain. Removal of a property is reported to the change subscriber as the path of the property that was removed.

feature4_structural_sharing.json.description: Updates apply structural sharing: when a nested property changes, only the objects along the path from the root to the changed property are rebuilt, while every object not on that path keeps its original reference. An identity probe captures the reference at a location before the mutation and compares it with the reference at the same location afterwards; it reports the location as preserved when the reference is unchanged and replaced when it was rebuilt. Objects on the changed path (the changed node's ancestors) are reported as replaced, and untouched sibling sub-trees are reported as preserved.

cases.expected_output:
changes=["store.a.x"]
identity store.a = replaced
identity store.b = preserved
identity store.list = preserved
snapshot={"a":{"x":99},"b":{"y":2},"list":[1,2]}

feature5_array_operations.json.description: Arrays held in the store are observable in the same way as objects. Assigning to an existing index replaces that element; assigning to the index one past the end appends a new element and grows the array's length accordingly. Elements that are themselves objects participate in structural sharing, so changing one element's property leaves the other elements' references untouched. A change to an element is reported with a path whose final segment is the element's index, and the array contents are observable in order through reads and the snapshot.

feature6_change_notification_order.json.description: Every mutation that flows through the store is reported to the change subscriber in the exact order the mutations are applied. Repeated changes to the same property each produce their own report rather than being collapsed, and a mix of assignments and removals is reported in interleaved order. The change subscriber receives one entry per mutation, each entry being the path of the affected property.

feature7_invalid_path_handling.json.description: Operations that require navigating through a non-existent or non-navigable location are rejected. Attempting to assign into a property whose parent object does not exist, or attempting to read a property of a primitive value as though it were an object, does not produce store contents; instead the adapter reports a single neutral error category indicating the path could not be resolved.

feature8_replace_store.json.description: The entire store can be replaced wholesale with a new set of contents while keeping the same store object identity. Replacing first removes every property currently present and then installs the properties of the replacement, so previously present keys disappear and the new keys become readable. Both the removals of the old properties and the additions of the new ones are reported to the change subscriber, removals first and additions after.
